target_objectives: Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encryption
Apply encryption concepts to different data states (at rest, in transit, in use)
supporting_objectives: Describe how encryption helps protect data

scenes.comment: Title slide: 'Let's Revisit: Encryption Types and Data States' with a subtle lock and key icon
scenes.speech: This video revisits two ideas from the lesson: the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and how those types are applied to protect data in different states.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] This video revisits two ideas from the lesson: the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and how those types are applied to protect data in different states.
scenes.gemini: [warmly] This video revisits two ideas from the lesson: the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and how those types are applied to protect data in different states.
scenes.html:
Lesson 9 · Re-teach

Encryption Types and Data States

scenes.comment: Simple side-by-side diagram showing plaintext going through encryption to scrambled data, then a key reversing it back to plaintext
scenes.speech: Quick recap. Encryption scrambles data so only someone with the right key can read it. Decryption reverses that process. And encryption must always be reversible - if there is no way to unlock it, it is not encryption, it is just data destruction.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Quick recap. Encryption scrambles data so only someone with the right key can read it. Decryption reverses that process. And encryption must always be reversible - if there is no way to unlock it, it is not encryption, it is just data destruction.
scenes.gemini: [clearly] Quick recap. Encryption scrambles data so only someone with the right key can read it. Decryption reverses that process. And encryption must always be reversible - if there is no way to unlock it, it is not encryption, it is just data destruction.
scenes.html: Encryption and Decryption

scenes.comment: Activity-style visual showing two students with a shared rule or key between them, evoking the Encryption Design Challenge
scenes.speech: In the Encryption Design Challenge, you invented your own method. Most methods you created used one rule or key that both people had to know. That is exactly the idea behind symmetric encryption.
scenes.elevenlabs: [happy] In the Encryption Design Challenge, you invented your own method. Most methods you created used one rule or key that both people had to know. That is exactly the idea behind symmetric encryption.
scenes.gemini: [warmly] In the Encryption Design Challenge, you invented your own method. Most methods you created used one rule or key that both people had to know. That is exactly the idea behind symmetric encryption.
scenes.html: Your Encryption Design Challenge

scenes.comment: Diagram showing one key used to both lock and unlock a message, with the label 'Symmetric Encryption: same key to lock and unlock'
scenes.speech: Symmetric encryption is when the same key is used to both lock and unlock a message. It is fast and efficient - great for protecting large amounts of data. The catch: both sides have to share the same secret key beforehand.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Symmetric encryption is when the same key is used to both lock and unlock a message. It is fast and efficient - great for protecting large amounts of data. The catch: both sides have to share the same secret key beforehand.
scenes.gemini: [deliberately] Symmetric encryption is when the same key is used to both lock and unlock a message. It is fast and efficient - great for protecting large amounts of data. [seriously] The catch: both sides have to share the same secret key beforehand.
scenes.html: Symmetric Encryption

scenes.comment: Diagram showing two keys: a public key shared broadly and a private key kept secret, with a mailbox visual illustrating the concept
scenes.speech: Asymmetric encryption uses two different keys. A public key that anyone can use to lock a message for you. And a private key only you have to unlock it. Think of a mailbox - anyone can drop mail in, but only you have the key to open it.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Asymmetric encryption uses two different keys. A public key that anyone can use to lock a message for you. And a private key only you have to unlock it. Think of a mailbox - anyone can drop mail in, but only you have the key to open it.
scenes.gemini: [clearly] Asymmetric encryption uses two different keys. A public key that anyone can use to lock a message for you. And a private key only you have to unlock it. [warmly] Think of a mailbox - anyone can drop mail in, but only you have the key to open it.
scenes.html: Asymmetric Encryption

scenes.comment: Visual of two people who have never met communicating securely over the internet, with a public key visible and a private key hidden
scenes.speech: This makes asymmetric encryption essential for the internet. You cannot always share a secret key with someone you have never met. Asymmetric encryption solves that: your public key is out there, so anyone can send you something secure - but only your private key can unlock it.
scenes.elevenlabs: [excited] This makes asymmetric encryption essential for the internet. You cannot always share a secret key with someone you have never met. Asymmetric encryption solves that: your public key is out there, so anyone can send you something secure - but only your private key can unlock it.
scenes.gemini: [thoughtfully] This makes asymmetric encryption essential for the internet. You cannot always share a secret key with someone you have never met. Asymmetric encryption solves that: your public key is out there, so anyone can send you something secure - but only your private key can unlock it.
scenes.html: Asymmetric Encryption Online

scenes.comment: Three-stage timeline showing a message moving from stored on a device, to traveling across a network, to being opened and read
scenes.speech: Now let's connect this to data states. Data does not stay in one place - it gets stored, sent, and opened. Protecting it means using the right type of encryption for each state: at rest, in transit, and in use.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Now let's connect this to data states. Data does not stay in one place - it gets stored, sent, and opened. Protecting it means using the right type of encryption for each state: at rest, in transit, and in use.
scenes.gemini: [curious] Now let's connect this to data states. Data does not stay in one place - it gets stored, sent, and opened. Protecting it means using the right type of encryption for each state: at rest, in transit, and in use.
scenes.html: Three Data States

scenes.comment: Visual of a hard drive or cloud storage icon with a symmetric key lock, labeled 'Data at Rest'
scenes.speech: Data at rest is information stored on a device - a phone, hard drive, or the cloud. Symmetric encryption is used here because it is fast and handles large amounts of data efficiently.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Data at rest is information stored on a device - a phone, hard drive, or the cloud. Symmetric encryption is used here because it is fast and handles large amounts of data efficiently.
scenes.gemini: [deliberately] Data at rest is information stored on a device - a phone, hard drive, or the cloud. Symmetric encryption is used here because it is fast and handles large amounts of data efficiently.
scenes.html: Data at Rest

scenes.comment: Visual of data packets moving across a network with asymmetric encryption establishing a connection, then symmetric encryption protecting the data flow, labeled 'Data in Transit'
scenes.speech: Data in transit is data moving across a network - like a message being sent. Asymmetric encryption establishes the secure connection first. Then symmetric encryption takes over to protect the data itself. Both types work together here.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Data in transit is data moving across a network - like a message being sent. Asymmetric encryption establishes the secure connection first. Then symmetric encryption takes over to protect the data itself. Both types work together here.
scenes.gemini: [deliberately] Data in transit is data moving across a network - like a message being sent. Asymmetric encryption establishes the secure connection first. Then symmetric encryption takes over to protect the data itself. [warmly] Both types work together here.
scenes.html: Data in Transit

scenes.comment: Visual of an open document on a screen being actively edited, labeled 'Data in Use', showing the file is decrypted while in use
scenes.speech: Data in use is data being actively read or edited - like an open document. It is usually decrypted so apps can work with it. When it is saved or sent, encryption kicks back in.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Data in use is data being actively read or edited - like an open document. It is usually decrypted so apps can work with it. When it is saved or sent, encryption kicks back in.
scenes.gemini: [clearly] Data in use is data being actively read or edited - like an open document. It is usually decrypted so apps can work with it. When it is saved or sent, encryption kicks back in.
scenes.html: Data in Use

scenes.comment: Visual emphasizing device security - a laptop with a lock icon and the phrase 'data is exposed while in use' highlighted
scenes.speech: This is why securing the device itself matters so much - the data is exposed while it is in use.
scenes.elevenlabs: [surprised] This is why securing the device itself matters so much - the data is exposed while it is in use.
scenes.gemini: [seriously] This is why securing the device itself matters so much - the data is exposed while it is in use.
scenes.html:
Data in Use

Secure the device.

The data is exposed while it is in use - which is why the device itself must be protected.

scenes.comment: Question of the Day slide displaying the question prominently: 'What makes encryption a powerful way to protect data?'
scenes.speech: Question of the Day: What makes encryption a powerful way to protect data? Think about how symmetric and asymmetric encryption each have strengths - and how applying the right type at the right data state is what makes a real system secure.
scenes.elevenlabs: [thoughtful] Question of the Day: What makes encryption a powerful way to protect data? Think about how symmetric and asymmetric encryption each have strengths - and how applying the right type at the right data state is what makes a real system secure.
scenes.gemini: [curious] Question of the Day: What makes encryption a powerful way to protect data? [thoughtfully] Think about how symmetric and asymmetric encryption each have strengths - and how applying the right type at the right data state is what makes a real system secure.
scenes.html:
Question of the Day

What makes encryption a powerful way to protect data?
